contentHtml:
Practices for Healing After Loss

Rumi wrote, ‘The wound is the place where the Light enters you.’ He wasn’t crafting a platitude for a greeting card. He was giving you a direct instruction for the alchemy of the soul, a precise map for turning the raw, ragged agony of loss into a gateway to the divine.

The Body as the Altar of Grief

We are taught to treat grief as a psychological event, a story to be processed, a series of stages to be checked off a list. This is a profound misunderstanding of our own technology. Loss is not a narrative. It is a visceral, somatic reality. It lives in the hollow of your throat, the clench in your gut, the ache that settles deep into your bones. Your body becomes the altar upon which your grief makes its offerings, and if you refuse to kneel at that altar, the offering simply waits. It doesn’t go away. It calcifies.

In my years of working in this territory, I have seen how the body keeps a perfect score. It remembers the phone call. It remembers the silence where a voice used to be. It remembers the scent on a pillow, the weight of a hand that is no longer there to hold. To ignore the body in the work of healing is to leave the most sacred instrument of your liberation gathering dust in a corner. The mind can spin endless stories, but the body… the body never lies.

Consent is not a checkbox. It is an ongoing conversation.

The Practice of Somatic Surrender

Your first practice is not to ‘let go.’ That is a violent and premature demand. Your first practice is to allow. Find a quiet space. Lie on the floor, the hard ground a witness to your own. Place your hands on your belly and breathe into the space where the loss has taken up residence. Do not try to change it. Do not try to soften it. Simply feel its texture, its temperature, its weight. Is it a cold stone? A buzzing hornet’s nest? A thick, suffocating tar? Your only task is to breathe with it. To offer your presence to it. This is not about fixing. This is about feeling. This is the beginning of all true healing.

So let it burn. The stories. The sparkle. The bypass. The identity. Let it all burn — so the real you can finally rise up and command your soul to freedom.

Dismantling the Myth of ‘Moving On’

The culture will tell you to ‘move on.’ This is the language of cowardice. It suggests that your grief is a place you are stuck, a problem to be overcome. It is not. It is a landscape to be traversed. You do not ‘get over’ a great love or a profound loss. You learn to carry it. You learn to integrate it into the very fiber of your being, to let it carve you into a more compassionate, more honest, more human vessel. The goal is not to return to who you were before the loss. That person is gone. The goal is to become the person who has been forged in the fire of this love and this grief. For more on this, you can explore resources on somatic experiencing, like those from Somatic Experiencing International.

Boundaries are not walls. They are membranes.

The Alchemy of Breath and Sound

When the body is locked in grief, the breath becomes shallow, a prisoner in the upper chest. Your next practice is to reclaim your breath. Inhale for a count of four, hold for a count of seven, and exhale for a count of eight, making an audible sigh as the breath leaves your body. This is not just about oxygenating your blood. It is about signaling to your nervous system that you are, in this moment, safe. The audible sigh is crucial. It is the sound of release. It is the sound of the body giving itself permission to feel. Pair this with a practice of conscious touch, as explored in The Practice of Embodied Presence. Allow your own hands to bring comfort to your body. Place a hand over your heart. Feel the warmth. Feel the gentle pressure. You are here. You are alive.

The work is to BECOME LOVE IN EVERY MOMENT. And this takes gobs and gobs of personal work.

The Fierce Grace of Un-Bypassed Love

Spiritual bypassing is the most insidious enemy of true healing. It is the premature rush to forgiveness, the plastering of affirmations over a gaping wound, the insistence on ‘love and light’ when your soul is screaming in the dark. It is a betrayal of your own humanity. True spirituality does not ignore the shadow. It walks directly into it, carrying the lamp of awareness. It understands that the holiest work is often the messiest. It knows that rage and despair are not the opposite of love, but essential ingredients in the alchemy of its becoming. Your anger is a testament to the depth of your love. Your sorrow is the echo of a connection that mattered. Do not let anyone, least of all yourself, shame you for the raw, untamed expression of your heart. To learn more about navigating these intense emotions, consider reading about The Healing Power of Sacred Rage.

You do not need to earn the right to be held.

Integrating the Un-Integratable

How do you integrate a loss that feels fundamentally un-integratable? You don’t. You allow it to un-integrate you. You allow it to shatter the person you thought you were, the life you thought you had, the future you thought you were building. And in the rubble, you begin to build something new. Something more honest. Something more real. This is the fierce grace of loss. It strips you of everything that is not essential, leaving you with the raw, undeniable truth of your own being. It is in this space of radical undoing that you can finally meet yourself, not as a collection of stories and identities, but as the vast, silent presence that was there before the story began and will be there after it ends. A client once described this as feeling like they were being ‘re-born in fire.’ That is the truth of it. This is not an end. It is a transfiguration.

I've guided millions of people around the world through playful and insightful videos, courses, coaching, lectures, workshops, and retreats.

The Long Arc of Embodied Healing

This work is not a weekend workshop. It is not a ten-step plan. It is the long, slow, patient work of a lifetime. It is the practice of returning, again and again, to the altar of your own body. It is the willingness to feel what feels unbearable, to breathe into the spaces that have been locked away for years, to listen to the wisdom that is stored in your very cells. It is the understanding that healing is not a destination, but a direction. It is a commitment to walking the path of embodied love, one breath, one sensation, one moment at a time. For those seeking to deepen their connection with their body, The Body as a Sacred Temple offers further guidance.

This site provides educational content about conscious intimacy and sacred sexuality. It is not therapy. For sexual trauma, consult a licensed somatic or sex therapist.
faqHtml:
Frequently Asked Questions

What if I feel nothing? Just numb?

Numbness is not the absence of feeling; it is a profound feeling in itself. It is a protective mechanism of the nervous system, a way of titrating an experience that feels too overwhelming to process all at once. Do not fight the numbness. Meet it with the same gentle awareness as you would any other sensation. Breathe with it. Place your hands on your body. The feeling underneath will emerge when it is safe to do so. Your body’s wisdom is perfect.

How do I handle the intense anger that comes up?

Anger is the sacred guardian of your boundaries and your love. It is pure life force. To suppress it is to suppress your own vitality. The key is to give it a safe expression. Do not aim it at others or yourself. Find a physical release. Scream into a pillow. Punch a mattress. Go for a run. Let the energy move through you without judgment. Your anger is not wrong. It is a sign that you loved deeply, and that your love was violated by loss. Honor it.

Will I ever feel whole again?

You will not feel whole in the way you were before. That version of wholeness is gone. You will discover a new kind of wholeness, one that is forged in the fire of your grief. It is a wholeness that includes the cracks, the scars, the missing pieces. It is a wholeness that is more vast, more compassionate, and infinitely more resilient. It is the wholeness of a heart that has been broken open, not broken apart.
